steps.title: Bottom-up cut enumeration with K = 4
steps.narrative: ABC's cut manager (Cut_NodeDoComputeCuts, also If_CutMerge for the FPGA mapper) computes cuts bottom-up. Each AND's cut set is built by merging cut sets of its two fanins. We'll walk the aoi3 AIG node by node.

panes.body: Definitions. A cut at node n is a set of nodes L such that every path from the PIs to n passes through L. A K-feasible cut has |L| ≤ K.

panes.title: Merge rule
panes.body: For each pair (cut0 from fanin0, cut1 from fanin1): merge = cut0 ∪ cut1. If |merge| > K, drop it.

panes.title: Plus the trivial cut
panes.body: Every node has the trivial cut {n} (the singleton-self), regardless of K.

steps.title: Step 1: PIs have only the trivial cut
steps.narrative: Cut sets at the PIs are seeded as { {a} }, { {b} }, { {c} }, { {d} }.

steps.title: Step 2: n6 = a & b — merge cut sets of a, b
steps.narrative: Pair every cut at a with every cut at b. Here only one pair: {a} ∪ {b} = {a, b} — size 2 ≤ K=4, keep. Plus the trivial cut {n6}.

steps.title: Step 3: n7 = a & d, n9 = c & d — same recipe
steps.narrative: Same merge-and-add-trivial pattern. Each yields exactly two cuts: the trivial self-cut and the union of the fanin trivial cuts.

steps.title: Step 4: n8 = NOR(n6, n7) — first interesting merge
steps.narrative: n8's fanins are n6 (with cuts {n6}, {a,b}) and n7 (with cuts {n7}, {a,d}). Pair every cut from each side:

panes.title: Cut set at n8 (after adding trivial)

steps.title: Step 5: f = AND(n8, ~n9) — bigger merge, K=4 starts to bite
steps.narrative: f's fanins are n8 (5 cuts) and n9 (2 cuts) → 5×2 = 10 candidate merges. With K=4, several get rejected.

steps.title: Step 6: containment filter (no-op on this circuit)
steps.narrative:
Cut_NodeFilter drops any cut C2 that is a (proper) superset of another cut C1 still in the set: anything covered by C1 is also covered by C2, but C1 has fewer leaves so it's strictly better. Same idea as redundant-implicant removal in SOP minimization.

For aoi3, none of f's surviving cuts contains another — the trivial {f} shares no element with the others, and the size-2 {n8,n9} isn't a subset of any larger cut (no other cut contains both n8 and n9). So no drop fires here. On bigger circuits with shared internal nodes the filter routinely halves the cut set.

steps.title: Step 7: optionally drop the worst cuts (nCutsMax cap)
steps.narrative:
Each cut manager has a per-node cap, nCutsMax (default 8 for rewrite's manager; varies elsewhere). With 10 cuts and a cap of 8, ABC drops the 2 cuts that score worst by some priority (typically size + leaf-fanout heuristic). On aoi3 with cap = 8 we'd lose 2 of the size-4 cuts.

For this walkthrough we'll keep all 10 to show the full set. Note that not every operator uses the same cap: refactor's cut manager uses much larger limits since it computes its own single recon-cut anyway.

panes.title: Final cut set at f (K=4, no nCutsMax cap applied here)

panes.body: These are the 10 cuts rewrite, refactor, and the FPGA if mapper see at f. Operators that need 4-leaf cuts (rewrite) iterate the 4-leaf rows; the LUT mapper picks the cut minimizing its current cost objective (delay / area-flow / exact area).
